channel.title: Arnaud - My site
channel.description: Recent content on Arnaud - My site

item.title: Brain Anomaly Detection
item.description: Non-AI-based Pixel-based a. Thresholding b. Clustering Edge-based An edge can be defined as a sudden change in intensity values in neighbouring pixels (in 2D). Various detection algorithms exist. Due to the shape of the brain and skull too many edges can be extracted. But what about using it after skull-stripping? https://kornia.readthedocs.io/en/0.5.2/filters.html#edge-detection https://github.com/kornia/kornia/issues/1181 It works well when used after skull-stripping: done in my repo MRI-tools/code/edge-based. And what about 3D neighbouring pixels?

item.title: MRI Tools
item.description: For MRI images handling several tools can be used, here I will briefly list the ones I try and use. Tools 1 Freesurfer (w/synthstrip) Software package developed my Martinos Center in Boston. Installation. Had to do a couple of export in my .zshrc to have the command line working. Link used for synthstrip Commands used: mri_synthstrip -i input -o stripped mri_synthstrip -i input.nii -o stripped.nii -m mask.nii --no-csf When run on frodo: -g for GPU.

item.title: Symmetry AD in MRI
item.description: Mohammad A. N. Al-Azawi (2021) Brain tumor/anomalies classification and segmentation can be of 3 types: Manual segmentation Semi-automatic segmentation; initialisation, feedback response, evaluation Fully automated segmentation Challenges: for CV diagnosis on brain regions Differences in shapes in patients’ brains. To which I can add differences in lesions. Image segmentation: tumor types, different brightness levels and imaging conditions Features extraction difficulties because of the natural brain shape Therefore: not possible to predict tumor status in someone’s brain based from other MRIs, hence the symmetry (left, right) suggestion.

item.title: Self-training outcome prediction
item.description: Ali et al. Optimised infant pipeline - but for fMRI teacher-student self-training DNN model. Outcome prediction. Most existing self-training models are designed for image classification with [[2DCNN|CNN]]. Paper cites top-performing models. Train DNN student model using labelled brain functional connectome data Used the previous model as a teacher to create pseudo labels for unlabeled connectome data. Combine labelled and unlabeled to train a new DNN. Iterate the procedure to obtain the best student model for the prediction task.

item.title: DL in infant MRI
item.description: 2019 - M.Mostapha, M.Styner Ressources for segmented MRIs given. Focus on the isointense age: 5-9 months. Higher potential for successful translation to other time points including the neonatal stage. Atlas-base segmentation techniques are challenged by registration errors in low contrast and intensity inhomogeneity from the infant population. Probabilistic methods and deformable-based models require large labelled datasets. Longitudinal approaches: require follow-up scans. Anatomy-guided tissue segmentation and toplogical correction framework: adopt a signed distance map with respect to the outer cortical surface as anatomical prior knowledge, and incorporate such prior information into the proposed framework to guide segmentation in ambiguous region.

item.title: Motor outcome prediction
item.description: 2020 - S.Saha et al. Motor outcome measurement with existing assessment. ~30 weeks PMA subjects. Uses 3D patches of MRI as input, 26x26x26 after gridsearch selection. Only take patches containing >50% brain matter. Used registration for skullstriping. Data augmentation: left-right flip, because any anatomical variation in the right hemisphere benefits the left and respectively. Model architecture, classification CNN: 3x [[2DCNN]] into 3 [[FC]]. A complete image was classified as abnormal if at least 27% of its patches predicted abnormal outcome.
